Project Checkpoint 2

Author

Hailey Ernest and Nicole Yee

Project Checkpoint 1

Data Context

This data describes the number of measles and rubella cases from 194 countries. It documents cases each month and year from January 2012 to July 2025. It is provided by the World Health Organization (WHO). Member states report this information to WHO as part of the WHO UNICEF annual data collection exercise. This data is used for investigation into monthly cases and is of interest for monitoring immunization.

Data Cleaning

The two data sets were cleaned separately. For the month dataset, column names were renamed using the janitor package. The content of each name is the same, it is just renamed in snake case. The data is also read in to ensure that all columns but region, country, and iso3 (country code) are numeric. No variables are created or modified otherwise.

The cases by year dataset is cleaned in a bit more detail. First, the first row becomes the column names. Then, column names are renamed using snake case so that they can easily be renamed to match the month dataset. The columns country, iso3, measles_total, measles_lab_confirmed, measles_epi_linked, measles_clinical, rubella_total, rubella_lab_confirmed, rubella_epi_linked, and rueball_clinical were renamed from member_state, iso_country_code, number_of_measles_cases_by_confirmation_method, na, na_2, na_3, number_of_rubella_cases_by_confirmation_method, na_4, na_5, and na_6 respectively. No other datatypes were changed and no variables were created.

Research Questions with Original Data

  1. Are certain countries showing more improvement in containing measles and rubella cases in recent years more than others?

  2. Is there a time of year where measles and rubella cases become more common globally?

  3. What proportion of suspected measles cases are discarded, and does this vary by region (in a single year)?

  4. Do Epidemiologically-linked cases make more of total confirmed cases compared to clinically-compatible cases, and does this differ between measles and rubella?

Research Questions with Supplemental Data

  1. Is there a consistently noticeable gap in monthly rubella cases between countries that have introduced the rubella vaccine and countries that have not?

  2. How long does it take for measles and rubella cases to noticeably drop after countries introduce vaccinations?

  3. Are countries with lower vaccination rates associated with higher measles incidence rates per million population?

  4. Do countries with lower GDP or weaker healthcare access have a higher proportion of clinically-compatible cases relative to lab-confirmed cases, meaning possibly limited diagnostic capacity?

Data Visualization Sketches


Project Checkpoint 2

Months with highest measles case count each year

Existing columns summary:

Custom function and iteration

# function to get the month of a year with the highest number of cases
max_month <- function(yr){
  cases_month |>
    filter(year == yr) |>
    group_by(month) |>
    summarize(num_measles = sum(measles_total, na.rm = TRUE)) |>
    slice_max(n=1, order_by = num_measles)
}

cases_year |>
  distinct(year) |>
  # apply function to each year in the data
  mutate(max_month = map(year, max_month)) |>
  # pull out columns from map
  unnest(max_month) |>
  # convert month abbreviations to month names
  mutate(month = month.name[month]) |>
  # make a gt table object
  gt() |>
  # rename and bold each column
  cols_label(
    year = md("**Year**"),
    month = md("**Month**"),
    num_measles = md("**Cases**")
  ) |>
  # add a table title
  tab_header(title = md("Time of Year with Increased Measles Cases Over Time")) |>
  # add a table caption
  tab_caption(caption = md("Months with the Highest Number of Cases.")) |>
  # bold column labels
  tab_style(
    style = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
    locations = cells_column_labels()
  )
Months with the Highest Number of Cases.
Time of Year with Increased Measles Cases Over Time
Year Month Cases

Proportion of suspected measles cases that are discarded by region (in a single year)

Existing columns summary:

Custom function

# function to get proportion of suspected cases that are discarded for each region in a given year
prop_discarded_by_region <- function(df, year_input) {
  df |>
    # filter data for selected year
    filter(year == year_input) |>
    # group data by region
    group_by(region) |>
    summarize(
      # total suspected measles cases for each region
      total_suspected = sum(measles_suspect, na.rm = TRUE),
      # total discarded cases for each region
      total_discarded = sum(discarded, na.rm = TRUE),
      # calculate proportion of discarded cases
      prop_discarded = total_discarded / total_suspected,
      .groups = "drop"
    )
}

prop_discarded_by_region(cases_month, 2022) |>
  # make a gt table
  gt() |>
  # format proportion column as percent (easier to understand)
  fmt_percent(columns = prop_discarded, decimals = 2) |>
  # rename columns
  cols_label(
    region = "Region",
    total_suspected = "Total Suspected Cases",
    total_discarded = "Total Discarded Cases",
    prop_discarded = "Proportion Discarded"
  ) |>
  # add a table title and caption
  tab_header(
    title = "Discarded Measles Cases by Region",
    subtitle = "Proportion of Discarded Cases in 2022"
  )|>
  # bold column labels
  tab_style(
    style = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
    locations = cells_column_labels()
  )
Discarded Measles Cases by Region
Proportion of Discarded Cases in 2022
Region Total Suspected Cases Total Discarded Cases Proportion Discarded
AFR 116498 51576 44.27%
AMR 12853 12805 99.63%
EMR 77939 37835 48.54%
EUR 6800 5922 87.09%
SEAR 138731 89239 64.33%
WPR 37021 35630 96.24%

Epidemiologically-linked case proportions by region and year

Modified columns summary:

Custom function and iteration

# function to get proportion of cases that are epidemiologically-linked for each year in one region
epi_proportions <- function(region) {
  cases_month |>
    filter(region == region) |>
    group_by(year) |>
    # get total counts for each year
    summarise(
      # measles total epidemiologically-linked and lab confirmed
              measles_epi_linked = sum(measles_epi_linked, na.rm = TRUE),
              measles_lab_confirmed = sum(measles_lab_confirmed, na.rm = TRUE),
      # rubella total epidemiologically-linked and lab confirmed     
              rubella_epi_linked = sum(rubella_epi_linked, na.rm = TRUE),
              rubella_lab_confirmed = sum(rubella_lab_confirmed, na.rm = TRUE)) |>
    # modify columns by calculating proportions
    mutate(prop_epi_measles = measles_epi_linked / measles_lab_confirmed,
           prop_epi_rubella = rubella_epi_linked / rubella_lab_confirmed) |>
    select(year,
           prop_epi_measles,
           prop_epi_rubella)
}

 cases_year |>
  distinct(region) |>
  # apply function to get proportions for each region
  mutate(epi_props = map(region, epi_proportions)) |>
  # get columns from returned map lists
  unnest(epi_props) |>
  # group by region so the table is divided by region
  group_by(region) |>
  # make a gt table
  gt() |>
   # format the proportion columns to be percents 
   fmt_percent(columns = matches("prop"), decimals = 1) |>
   # rename and bold column names
    cols_label(
      region = md("**Region**"),
      year = md("**Year**"),
      prop_epi_measles = md("**Epidemiologically-linked measles cases**"),
      prop_epi_rubella = md("**Epidemiologically-linked rubella cases**")
    ) |>
   # add a table title
   tab_header(
     title = md("Proportion of Confirmed Cases that are Epidemiologically-linked"),
   ) |>
   # add a table caption
   tab_caption(
     caption = md("Changes in each region's confirmed case makeup over time.")
   )
Changes in each region’s confirmed case makeup over time.

Measles cases for years following vaccine introduction

Modified columns summary:

Iteration

# get first year with measles vaccine coverage above 0 for each country
measles_intro <- measles_vax |>
  filter(!is.na(coverage), coverage > 0) |>
  group_by(name) |>
  summarize(
    vax_year = min(year),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# join vaccine introduction year to monthly measles cases
measles_cases_with_vax <- cases_month |>
  select(region, country, year, month, measles_total) |>
  # join vaccine introduction year by country
  left_join(measles_intro, by = c("country" = "name")) |>
  # create modified column for years since vaccine introduction
  mutate(
    years_since_vax = year - vax_year
  )

# use map to get average measles cases by years since vaccine introduction for each country

# Note: when the country’s introduction to vaccine occurred that is what calculated the average on that year, so 1st year is all the countries' averages who were introduced after 1 year (no matter the starting year - one country could have it be in 2012 and another in 2020). And coverage above 0 is the dataset's measure of when there were more than 0 vaccines that year for that country

measles_country_patterns <- measles_cases_with_vax |>
  distinct(country) |>
  filter(!is.na(country)) |>
  mutate(case_pattern = map(country, \(country_input) {
    measles_cases_with_vax |>
      # filter data to one country
      filter(country == country_input,
             !is.na(years_since_vax),
             years_since_vax >= 0) |>
      group_by(years_since_vax) |>
      summarize(
        avg_cases = mean(measles_total, na.rm = TRUE),
        .groups = "drop"
      )
  })) |>
  # columns pulled from mapped results
  unnest(case_pattern)

# summarize across countries for average pattern after vaccine introduction
measles_after_vax <- measles_country_patterns |>
  group_by(years_since_vax) |>
  summarize(
    avg_cases = mean(avg_cases, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# make a gt table
measles_after_vax |>
  gt() |>
  # format average cases
  fmt_number(columns = avg_cases, decimals = 1) |>
  # rename columns
  cols_label(
    years_since_vax = "Years Since Vaccine Introduction",
    avg_cases = "Average Measles Cases"
  ) |>
  # add a table title and subtitle
  tab_header(
    title = "Change in Measles Cases After Vaccine Introduction"
  ) |>
  tab_caption(
    caption = "Countries are aligned by the first year (coverage above 0) and averaged across all countries"
  ) |>
  # bold column labels
  tab_style(
    style = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
    locations = cells_column_labels()
  )
Countries are aligned by the first year (coverage above 0) and averaged across all countries
Change in Measles Cases After Vaccine Introduction
Years Since Vaccine Introduction Average Measles Cases

# use map to get average measles cases by years since vaccine introduction for each region
measles_region_patterns <- measles_cases_with_vax |>
  # get distinct regions from the data
  distinct(region) |>
  # remove missing region values
  filter(!is.na(region)) |>
  # apply function to get case patterns for each region
  mutate(case_pattern = map(region, \(region_input) {
    measles_cases_with_vax |>
      # filter data to one region
      filter(region == region_input,
             !is.na(years_since_vax),
             years_since_vax >= 0) |>
      group_by(years_since_vax) |>
      summarize(
        avg_cases = mean(measles_total, na.rm = TRUE),
        .groups = "drop"
      )
  })) |>
  # get columns from returned map lists
  unnest(case_pattern)

measles_region_patterns |>
  # group by region so the table is divided by region
  group_by(region) |>
  # make a gt table
  gt() |>
  # format average cases
  fmt_number(columns = avg_cases, decimals = 1) |>
  # rename and bold column names
  cols_label(
    region = "Region",
    years_since_vax = "Years Since Vaccine Introduction",
    avg_cases = "Average Measles Cases"
  ) |>
  # add a table title and subtitle
  tab_header(
    title = "Change in Measles Cases After Vaccine Introduction"
  ) |>
  tab_caption(
    caption = "Year 0 represents the first year a country has official coverage 
    above 0; values are averaged within each region"
  ) |>
  # bold column labels
  tab_style(
    style = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
    locations = cells_column_labels()
  )
Year 0 represents the first year a country has official coverage above 0; values are averaged within each region
